lesson.title: Number Types
lesson.body: An integer is any whole number with no decimal part: …, −2, −1, 0, 1, 2, … Rational numbers can be written as a fraction p/q. Irrational numbers (like √2 and π) cannot. The real number line contains both rational and irrational numbers.

rooms.question: Which of these is an integer?
rooms.explanation: An integer is a whole number with no fractional part. −7 qualifies. 0.5 has a decimal, √2 ≈ 1.414..., and π ≈ 3.14159... — none of these are whole.

rooms.question: Where does -3 sit on the number line relative to -1?
rooms.explanation: On the number line, numbers increase left to right. −3 is to the left of −1, so −3 < −1. A larger negative magnitude means a smaller value.

rooms.question: Which set contains only rational numbers?
steps: \text{A rational number equals } \frac{p}{q} \text{ for integers } p, q
rooms.explanation: 0.5 = 1/2, −3 = −3/1, and 2/7 are all ratios of integers, so they are rational. π and e are irrational (no fraction equals them exactly). √2 and √3 are also irrational.

rooms.question: What is |-8|?
steps: \text{Absolute value = distance from 0 on the number line}
rooms.explanation: Absolute value measures distance from zero, which is always non-negative. The distance from −8 to 0 is 8 steps, so |−8| = 8. It is not −8 (distances can't be negative) and not 64 (that would be (−8)²).

rooms.question: Order these number sets from smallest to largest (fewest numbers to most):
rooms.explanation: ℕ ⊂ ℤ ⊂ ℚ ⊂ ℝ. Each set is strictly larger: integers add negatives and zero to naturals; rationals add fractions; reals add irrationals like π and √2.

boss.name: The Numeromancer
boss.taunt: Your number sense is pathetically weak.

questions.question: Which of the following integers cannot be written as a ratio of two integers?
questions.explanation: Every integer n can be written as n/1, a valid ratio of two integers with q ≠ 0. So −100, 0, and 17 are all rational. There is no integer that fails to be rational — the integers are a subset of the rationals.

questions.question: The number 0.333... (repeating) is:
questions.explanation: A decimal is rational if it terminates OR repeats. 0.333... = 1/3 exactly. Irrationals like π also never end, but they never repeat a pattern — that is the key difference.

questions.question: Which number belongs to the reals but NOT to the rationals?
questions.explanation: √11 ≈ 3.3166… is irrational — it cannot be expressed as p/q — yet it exists on the real number line. The others: 7/2 = 3.5 (rational), √9 = 3 (a rational integer), and 0.6̄ = 2/3 (repeating decimal, hence rational).
